q: For an elementary chemical reaction, A2 ⇄k-1k12A , the expression for dAdt is:
question: For an elementary chemical reaction, A2 ⇄k-1k12A , the expression for dAdt is:
options: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mfenced open="[" close="]" separators="|"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfenced><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub><msup><mfenced open="[" close="]" separators="|"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup></math>

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mfenced open="[" close="]" separators="|"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfenced><mo>-</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub><msup><mfenced open="[" close="]" separators="|"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup></math>

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mfenced open="[" close="]" separators="|"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfenced><mo>+</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub><msup><mfenced open="[" close="]" separators="|"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup></math>

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mfenced open="[" close="]" separators="|"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfenced><mo>-</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub><msup><mfenced open="[" close="]" separators="|"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup></math>
solution:
For the reaction, A2→K12A

rf=-dA2dt=12dAdt1=K1A2

dAdt1=2K1A2

For the reaction, 2A→k-1 A2  

rb=-12dAdt2=K-1A2

dAdt2=-2K-1A2

Hence, the overall rate is given by:

dAdt=2k1A2-2k-1A2

q:
A sample of milk splits after 60min . at 300K and after 40min . at 400K when the population of Iactobacillus acidophilus in it doubles. The activation energy (in kJ/mol ) for this process is closest to ______________.
(Given, R=8.3Jmol-1K-1,ln23=0.4,e-3=4.0 )
question:
A sample of milk splits after 60min . at 300K and after 40min . at 400K when the population of Iactobacillus acidophilus in it doubles. The activation energy (in kJ/mol ) for this process is closest to ______________.
(Given, R=8.3Jmol-1K-1,ln23=0.4,e-3=4.0 )
solution:
lnk2k1=EaR1T11T2
ln6040=Ea8.3×100400×300
ln32×8.3×1200=Ea
⟹Ea=0.4×8.3×1200
⟹Ea=3984Jmol.
⟹Ea=3.984 k Jmol.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Consider the following statements related to temperature dependence of rate constants.
Identify the correct statements,
A. The Arrhenius equation holds true only for an elementary homogenous reaction.
B. The unit of A is same as that of k in Arrhenius equation.
C. At a given temperature, a low activation energy means a fast reaction.
D. A and Ea as used in Arrhenius equation depend on temperature.
E. When $\mathrm{Ea} \gg \mathrm{RT}$. A and Ea become interdependent.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Consider the following statements related to temperature dependence of rate constants.
Identify the correct statements,
A. The Arrhenius equation holds true only for an elementary homogenous reaction.
B. The unit of A is same as that of k in Arrhenius equation.
C. At a given temperature, a low activation energy means a fast reaction.
D. A and Ea as used in Arrhenius equation depend on temperature.
E. When $\mathrm{Ea} \gg \mathrm{RT}$. A and Ea become interdependent.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
Arrhenious equation hold true for elementary as well as complex reactions.
Unit of A is same as unit of k. Rate of reaction is high if activation energy is low,
A and Ea are temperature independent.

Concentration of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> changes from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mmol</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>dm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mmol</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>dm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Rate of appearance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">D</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math> times the rate of disappearance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> which is twice the rate of disappearance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>. The rate of appearance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">D</mi></math> has been experimentally determined to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mmol</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>dm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> Therefore the rate of reaction is____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mmol</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>dm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> (Nearest Integer)
question: For a given chemical reaction <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">γ</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>+</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">γ</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>→</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">γ</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>+</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">γ</mi><mn>4</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">D</mi></math>. Concentration of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> changes from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mmol</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>dm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mmol</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>dm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Rate of appearance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">D</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math> times the rate of disappearance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> which is twice the rate of disappearance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>. The rate of appearance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">D</mi></math> has been experimentally determined to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mmol</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>dm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> Therefore the rate of reaction is____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mmol</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>dm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> (Nearest Integer)
solution:
dcdt=20-1010=1milimole/dm3sec

γ1A+γ2B→γ3C+γ4D

dDdt=1.5-ddtB=9 milimole/dm3 sec

-ddtB=2-ddtA

Rate =-1γ1dAdt=-1γ2dBdt=1γ3dCdt=1γ4dDdt

(i) dDdt=γ4γ2-dBdt=1.5-ddtB

γ4γ2=1.5

(ii) γ2γ1-dAdt=-ddtB=2-ddtA

γ2γ1=2

(iii) dCdt=γ3γ4dDdt

1=γ3γ4×9

γ4γ3=9

So, if

 γ3 = 1 than γ4 = 9γ2 = 6 and γ1 = 3Rate of reaction = 1γ3d[C]dt = 1 m mol dm-3 s-1

subject: Chemistry
q: $\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_5$ decomposes to $\mathrm{NO}_2$ and $\mathrm{O}_2$ and follows first order kinetics. After $50$ minutes, the pressure inside the vessel increases from $50 \mathrm{~mm} \mathrm{~Hg}$ to $87.5 \mathrm{~mm} \mathrm{~Hg}$. The pressure of the gaseous mixture after $100$ minutes at constant temperature will be ______.
question: $\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_5$ decomposes to $\mathrm{NO}_2$ and $\mathrm{O}_2$ and follows first order kinetics. After $50$ minutes, the pressure inside the vessel increases from $50 \mathrm{~mm} \mathrm{~Hg}$ to $87.5 \mathrm{~mm} \mathrm{~Hg}$. The pressure of the gaseous mixture after $100$ minutes at constant temperature will be ______.
solution:
$\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_5 \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NO}_2+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_2$
$\begin{array}{llll}\text { At } \mathrm{t}=0 & 50 & 0 & 0 \\ \text { At } \mathrm{t}=50 \mathrm{~min} & 50-\mathrm{p}_1 & 2 \mathrm{p}_1 & \frac{\mathrm{p}_1}{2}\end{array}$
Total pressure at $50$ minutes
$\begin{aligned}
&=50-\mathrm{p}_1+2 \mathrm{p}_1+\frac{\mathrm{p}_1}{2}=87.5 \\
&50+\frac{3 \mathrm{p}_1}{2}=87.5 \\
&\frac{3 \mathrm{p}_1}{2}=37.5 \\
&\therefore \quad \mathrm{p}_1=\frac{37.5 \times 2}{3}=25
\end{aligned}$
At $\mathrm{t}=100 \mathrm{~min} \quad 50-\mathrm{p}_2 \quad 2 \mathrm{p}_2 \quad \frac{\mathrm{p}_2}{2}$
$50$ minutes is half life period
For $100$ minutes i.e. for $2$ half lives $50-p_2=12.5$
$\therefore \mathrm{p}_2=37.5 \mathrm{~mm} \text { of } \mathrm{Hg}$
Total pressure at $100$ minutes
$=50-\mathrm{p}_2+2 \mathrm{p}_2+\frac{\mathrm{p}_2}{2}$
$=50+\frac{3 \mathrm{p}_2}{2}=50+\frac{3}{2} \times 37.5$
$=50+56.25$
$=106.25 \mathrm{~mm}$ of $\mathrm{Hg}$

subject: Chemistry
q: A radioactive isotope having a half - life period of $3$ days was received after $12$ days. If $3 \mathrm{~g}$ of the isotope is left in the container, what would be the initial mass of the isotope ?
question: A radioactive isotope having a half - life period of $3$ days was received after $12$ days. If $3 \mathrm{~g}$ of the isotope is left in the container, what would be the initial mass of the isotope ?
solution:
Given $t_{1 / 2}=3$
Total time $\mathrm{T}=12$
No. of half lives $(n)=\frac{12}{3}=4$
$\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^n=\frac{\mathrm{N}}{\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{o}}}$
$\therefore \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^4=\frac{3}{\mathrm{~N}_{\mathrm{o}}}$
$\frac{3}{\mathrm{~N}_{\mathrm{o}}}=\frac{1}{16}$
$\mathrm{~N}_{\mathrm{o}}=48 \mathrm{~g}$

subject: Chemistry
q:
Consider the reaction $aX \rightarrow bY$, for which the rate constant at $30°C$ is $1 \times 10^{-3}$ mol$^{-1}$ L s$^{-1}$. Which of the following statements are true?
A. When concentration of 'X' is increased to four times, the rate of reaction becomes $16$ times.
B. The reaction is a second order reaction.
C. The half-life period is independent of the concentration of X.
D. Decomposition of $N_2 O_5$ is an example of the above reaction.
E. $\ln\dfrac{[R_o]}{[R]}$ vs time is valid for the above reaction.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Consider the reaction $aX \rightarrow bY$, for which the rate constant at $30°C$ is $1 \times 10^{-3}$ mol$^{-1}$ L s$^{-1}$. Which of the following statements are true?
A. When concentration of 'X' is increased to four times, the rate of reaction becomes $16$ times.
B. The reaction is a second order reaction.
C. The half-life period is independent of the concentration of X.
D. Decomposition of $N_2 O_5$ is an example of the above reaction.
E. $\ln\dfrac{[R_o]}{[R]}$ vs time is valid for the above reaction.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
options: A, B, D and E Only
solution:
The unit of the rate constant $k$ is mol$^{-1}$ L s$^{-1}$.

The general unit of the rate constant for an $n$-th order reaction is (mol L$^{-1}$)$^{1-n}$ s$^{-1}$.

Equating the powers of the concentration units, we get $1-n = -1 \Rightarrow n = 2$.

Thus, the reaction is a second-order reaction. Statement B is correct.

For a second-order reaction, Rate $= k[X]^2$. When the concentration of $X$ is increased to $4$ times, the new rate is $k(4[X])^2 = 16k[X]^2$, which is $16$ times the original rate. Statement A is correct.

The half-life of a second-order reaction is $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{1}{k[X]_0}$, which depends inversely on the initial concentration. Statement C is incorrect.

The decomposition of $N_2O_5$ is a well-known first-order reaction. Statement D is incorrect.

The equation $\ln\dfrac{[R_o]}{[R]} = kt$ represents the integrated rate law for a first-order reaction, not a second-order reaction. Statement E is incorrect.

Therefore, only statements A and B are correct.

Answer: A and B Only

question: At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>518</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the rate of decomposition of a sample of gaseous acetaldehyde, initially at a pressure of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>363</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>Torr</mi></math> was <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.00</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>Torr</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> when <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">%</mi></math> had reacted and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.50</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>Torr</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> when 33% had reacted. solution:
Rate∝concentrationn

Let the initial concentration be 1 M.

According to the question,

Concentration  Rate0.95 M10.67 M 0.5

Substituting in the general rate expression,

0.950.67n=10.5

⇒n=2

Relation between rate constant (k) and absolute temperature (T):

Arrhenius equation: k =A e-EaRT

The value of rate constant (k) will increase exponentially with rise in temperature for all type of reactions.

subject: Chemistry
q: Pre-exponential factors of two different reactions of same order are identical. Let activation energy of first reaction exceeds the activation energy of second reaction by $20 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. If $\mathrm{k}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{k}_{2}$ are the rate constants of first and second reaction respectively at 300 K, then $\ln \frac{\mathrm{k}_{2}}{\mathrm{k}_{1}}$ will be $\_\_\_\_$. (nearest integer) $\left[\mathrm{R}=8.3 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right]$
question: Pre-exponential factors of two different reactions of same order are identical. Let activation energy of first reaction exceeds the activation energy of second reaction by $20 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. If $\mathrm{k}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{k}_{2}$ are the rate constants of first and second reaction respectively at 300 K, then $\ln \frac{\mathrm{k}_{2}}{\mathrm{k}_{1}}$ will be $\_\_\_\_$. (nearest integer) $\left[\mathrm{R}=8.3 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right]$
solution:
Given: $A_1 = A_2$, $E_{a1} = E_{a2} + 20$ kJ/mol, T = 300 K, R = 8.3 J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹
Using Arrhenius equation: $k = Ae^{-E_a/RT}$
$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = \frac{e^{-E_{a2}/RT}}{e^{-E_{a1}/RT}} = e^{(E_{a1} - E_{a2})/RT}$
$E_{a1} - E_{a2} = 20$ kJ/mol = 20000 J/mol
$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = e^{\frac{20000}{8.3 \times 300}} = e^{\frac{20000}{2490}}$
$\ln\frac{k_2}{k_1} = \frac{20000}{2490} = 8.03 \approx 8$

Given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>31</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
question: Catalyst A reduces the activation energy for a reaction by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kJ</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math>. The ratio of rate constants, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>Catalysed</mi></mrow><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>Uncatalysed</mi></mrow></mfrac></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></msup></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is____[nearest integer] [Assume that the pre-exponential factor is same in both the cases. Given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>31</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
solution:
Arrhenius equation for the rate constant of a reaction in terms of energy of activation is given by

K=Ae-EaRT

Kcatalyst=AeEa'RT

Kuncatalysed=Ae-EaRT

KcatalystKUncatalysed=eEa-Ea'RT=e10×10008.314×300=e4

subject: Chemistry
q: The rate constants for decomposition of acetaldehyde have been measured over the temperature range 700-1000 K. The data has been analysed by plotting lnk vs 103 T graph. The value of activation energy for the reaction is kJmol-1. (Nearest integer) (Given : R=8.31 JK-1 mol-1)
question: The rate constants for decomposition of acetaldehyde have been measured over the temperature range 700-1000 K. The data has been analysed by plotting lnk vs 103 T graph. The value of activation energy for the reaction is kJmol-1. (Nearest integer) (Given : R=8.31 JK-1 mol-1)
solution:
k=Ae-EaRT

lnk=lnA-EaRT

lnk=lnA+-Ea1000R1000T

Slope =-Ea1000R=-18.5

Ea=18.5×1000×8.31

=153.735×103 J

=154 KJ

subject: Chemistry
q:
For the reaction, $2 \mathrm{~N}_2 \mathrm{O}_5 \rightarrow 4 \mathrm{NO}_2+\mathrm{O}_2$, the rate equation can be expressed in two ways $-\frac{\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_5\right]}{\mathrm{dt}}=\mathrm{k}\left[\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_5 \quad\right] n \mathrm{nd}$ $+\frac{\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{NO}_2\right]}{\mathrm{dt}}=\mathrm{k}^{\prime}\left[\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_5\right]$
$\mathrm{k}$ and $\mathrm{k}^{\prime}$ are related as:
question:
For the reaction, $2 \mathrm{~N}_2 \mathrm{O}_5 \rightarrow 4 \mathrm{NO}_2+\mathrm{O}_2$, the rate equation can be expressed in two ways $-\frac{\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_5\right]}{\mathrm{dt}}=\mathrm{k}\left[\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_5 \quad\right] n \mathrm{nd}$ $+\frac{\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{NO}_2\right]}{\mathrm{dt}}=\mathrm{k}^{\prime}\left[\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_5\right]$
$\mathrm{k}$ and $\mathrm{k}^{\prime}$ are related as:
solution:
Rate of disappearance of reactant $=$ Rate of appearance of products
$$
\begin{aligned}
&-\frac{1}{2} \frac{\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_5\right]}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{1}{4} \frac{\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{NO}_2\right]}{\mathrm{dt}} \\
&\frac{1}{2} k\left[\mathrm{~N}_2 \mathrm{O}_5\right]=\frac{1}{4} k^{\prime}\left[\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_5\right] \\
&\frac{k}{2}=\frac{k^{\prime}}{4} \\
&\therefore k^{\prime}=2 k
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Chemistry
q:
The temperature at which the rate constants of the given below two gaseous reactions become equal is $\_\_\_\_$ K. (Nearest integer)
$\mathrm{X} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Y} \quad \mathrm{k}_{1}=10^{6} e^{\frac{-30000}{\mathrm{~T}}}$
$\mathrm{P} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Q} \quad \mathrm{k}_{2}=10^{4} e^{\frac{-24000}{\mathrm{~T}}}$
Given: $\ln 10=2.303$
question:
The temperature at which the rate constants of the given below two gaseous reactions become equal is $\_\_\_\_$ K. (Nearest integer)
$\mathrm{X} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Y} \quad \mathrm{k}_{1}=10^{6} e^{\frac{-30000}{\mathrm{~T}}}$
$\mathrm{P} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Q} \quad \mathrm{k}_{2}=10^{4} e^{\frac{-24000}{\mathrm{~T}}}$
Given: $\ln 10=2.303$
solution:
To find the temperature at which $k_1 = k_2$,
set the rate constant equations equal: $10^6 e^{-30000/T} = 10^4 e^{-24000/T}$.
Dividing both sides by $10^4$: $100 = e^{-24000/T + 30000/T} = e^{6000/T}$.
Taking natural logarithm: $\ln(100) = \frac{6000}{T}$.
Since $\ln(100) = 2\ln(10) = 2 \times 2.303 = 4.606$: $4.606 = \frac{6000}{T}$.
Solving for T: $T = \frac{6000}{4.606} = 1302.5$ K.
Rounding to the nearest integer gives T = 1303 K.

subject: Chemistry
q: A and B are two substances undergoing radioactive decay in a container. The half life of A is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi>min</mi></math> and that of B is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>min</mi></math>. If the initial concentration of B is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> times that of A and they both start decaying at the same time, how much time will it take for the concentration of both of them to be same? ________min.
question: A and B are two substances undergoing radioactive decay in a container. The half life of A is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi>min</mi></math> and that of B is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>min</mi></math>. If the initial concentration of B is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> times that of A and they both start decaying at the same time, how much time will it take for the concentration of both of them to be same? ________min.
solution:
For a first order reaction:
⇒[A]t=[A]0e-kt

For A : Let [A]t be y and [A]0 be x
For a first order reaction:
 ∴k=ln2t1/2=ln215 min

⇒y=xe-kt

=xe-ln215t    ....(1)

Similarly For B:
 [B]t=[B]0e-kt

Let [B]t=y;[B]0=4x;k=ln2t1/2=ln25 min

⇒y=4xe-ln25t      ....(2)
Equating equation(1) and (2) we get:

⇒xe-ln215t=4xe-ln25t

⇒t×ln 25-ln 215=ln 4

⇒t×ln215-115=2 ln2

⇒etln 25-ln 215=4

⇒t=15 min

subject: Chemistry
q: A radioactive element gets spilled over the floor of a room. Its half-life period is $30$ days. If the initial activity is ten times the permissible value, after how many days will it be safe to enter the room?
question: A radioactive element gets spilled over the floor of a room. Its half-life period is $30$ days. If the initial activity is ten times the permissible value, after how many days will it be safe to enter the room?
solution:
Activity $\left(-\frac{\mathrm{dN}}{\mathrm{dt}}\right) \propto \mathrm{N}$
$\mathrm{N}=\mathrm{N}_{\circ}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{\mathrm{n}}$
$\frac{\mathrm{N}}{\mathrm{N}_{\circ}}=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{\mathrm{n}}$
$\frac{1}{10}=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{\mathrm{n}} \Rightarrow 10=2^{\mathrm{n}}$
$\log 10=\mathrm{nlog} 2$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=\frac{1}{0.301}=3.32$
$\mathrm{t}=\mathrm{n} \times \mathrm{t}_{112}$
$=3.32 \times 30=99.6 \text { days }$
Hence, (D) is correct.

question:

Ag→2 Bg+Cg

S. No                 Time/s                 Total pressure/(atm)

 2.                      115                     0.28

The rate constant of the reaction is _______ ×10-2s-1 (nearest integer)
solution:
t=0t=115 sec. Ag0.10.1-x →2Bg2x+Cg2x

0.1+2x = 0.28

2x=0.18

x=0.09

K=1115ln0.10.1-0.09

=0.0200sec-1

=2×10-2sec-1

q: Consider the following plots of log of rate constant $\mathrm{k}(\log \mathrm{k})$ vs $\frac{1}{\mathrm{~T}}$ for three different reactions. The correct order of activation energies of these reactions is
question: Consider the following plots of log of rate constant $\mathrm{k}(\log \mathrm{k})$ vs $\frac{1}{\mathrm{~T}}$ for three different reactions. The correct order of activation energies of these reactions is
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{K}=\mathrm{A} \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{E} a \mathrm{RT}} \\
& \operatorname{logk}=\log \mathrm{A}-\frac{\mathrm{Ea}}{2.303 \mathrm{RT}}
\end{aligned}$
For graph between logk with $\frac{1}{\mathrm{~T}}$
$\mid \text { Slope of curve } \left\lvert\,=\frac{\mathrm{Ea}}{2.303 \mathrm{R}}\right.$
From given graph
Magnitude of slope $\Rightarrow(2) \gt (1) \gt (3)$
Hence $\mathrm{Ea}_2 \gt \mathrm{Ea}_1 \gt \mathrm{Ea}_3$

q: A bacterial infection in an internal wound grows as <math><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">'</mi></mrow></msup><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi><mo>⁡</mo><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mo>)</mo></math> , where the time <math><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></math> is in hours. A dose of antibiotic, taken orally, needs 1 hour to reach the wound. Once it reaches there, the bacterial population goes down as <math><mfrac><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> . What will be the plot of <math><mfrac><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></mrow></mfrac></math> vs <math><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></math> after <math><mn>1</mn></math> hour?
question: A bacterial infection in an internal wound grows as <math><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">'</mi></mrow></msup><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi><mo>⁡</mo><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mo>)</mo></math> , where the time <math><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></math> is in hours. A dose of antibiotic, taken orally, needs 1 hour to reach the wound. Once it reaches there, the bacterial population goes down as <math><mfrac><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> . solution:
From 0 to 1 hour, N'=N0et
From 1 hour onwards dNdt=-5N2
So at t=1 hour, N'=eN0
dNdt=-5N2
∫eN0NN-2dN=-5∫1tdt
1N-1eN0=5t-1
N0N-1e=5N0(t-1)
N0N=5N0(t-1)+1e
N0N=5N0t+1e-5N0

subject: Chemistry
q:
A→B

The above reaction is of zero order. Half life of this reaction is 50 min. The time taken for the concentration of A to reduce to one-fourth of its initial value is min . (Nearest integer)
question:
A→B

The above reaction is of zero order. Half life of this reaction is 50 min. The time taken for the concentration of A to reduce to one-fourth of its initial value is min . (Nearest integer)
solution:
The integrated rate equation of zero order reaction is

kt=A0-At

Assume reaction starts with 1 mole A

t1/2=a2k,K=12×50

For 75% completion

A0=a, At=a4

a-a4=kt

t=34ak=34×100a=75 min

q:
Two reactions A1 and A2 have identical pre-exponential factors. The activation energy of A1 is more than A2 by 10 kJ mol-1 . If k1 and k2 are the rate constants for reactions A1 and A2, respectively at 300 K, then ln⁡k2k1 is equal to

question:
Two reactions A1 and A2 have identical pre-exponential factors. The activation energy of A1 is more than A2 by 10 kJ mol-1 . If k1 and k2 are the rate constants for reactions A1 and A2, respectively at 300 K, then ln⁡k2k1 is equal to

R=8.314 J mol-1K-1
solution:
From Arrhenius equation

k=Ae-Ea/RT

k1=Ae-Ea1/RT

k2=Ae-Ea2/RT

lnk2k1=eEa1-Ea2/RT

ln⁡k2k1=Ea1-Ea2RT=10×10008.314×300=4

subject: Chemistry
q:
Consider the following data for the given reaction
2HI(g)→H2( g)+I2( g)

HImolL-1                     0.005         0.01         0.02

Rate molL-1 s-1      7.5×10-4      3.0×10-3       1.2×10-2

The order of the reaction is __________.
question:
Consider the following data for the given reaction
2HI(g)→H2( g)+I2( g)

HImolL-1                     0.005         0.01         0.02

Rate molL-1 s-1      7.5×10-4      3.0×10-3       1.2×10-2

The order of the reaction is __________.
solution:
The initial concentrations of HI as 0.005, 0.01, and 0.02 mol/L, respectively, and the corresponding initial rates as given:

HImolL-1                     0.005         0.01         0.02

Rate molL-1 s-1      7.5×10-4      3.0×10-3       1.2×10-2

 For the reaction, rate law is
rate=kHIP
P is the order of the reaction.
From the given data
7.5×10-4=k0.05P-------(1)
3×10-3=k0.01P  --------(2)
1.2×10-2=k0.02P ---------(3)
On dividing Equation (3) by equation (2)
12×10-33×10-3=k0.02Pk0.01P
⇒2P=22
⇒P=2

subject: Chemistry
q:
For the reaction, 2A+B→ products, when the concentration of A and B both were doubled, the rate of the reaction increased from 0.3 mol L-1s-1 to 2.4 mol L-1s-1.  When the concentration of A alone is doubled, the rate increased from 0.3 mol L-1s-1 to 0.6 mol L-1s-1.

Which one of the following statements is correct?
question:
For the reaction, 2A+B→ products, when the concentration of A and B both were doubled, the rate of the reaction increased from 0.3 mol L-1s-1 to 2.4 mol L-1s-1.  When the concentration of A alone is doubled, the rate increased from 0.3 mol L-1s-1 to 0.6 mol L-1s-1.

Which one of the following statements is correct?
options: Order of the reaction with respect to <math><mi>B</mi></math>  is  <math><mn>2</mn></math>
Total order of the reaction is <math><mn>4</mn></math>
Order of the reaction with respect to <math><mi>A</mi></math>  is <math><mn>2</mn></math>
Order of the reaction with respect to <math><mi>B</mi></math> is <math><mn>1</mn></math>
solution:
2A+B→C

Initial r1=kAxBy=0.3 m/sec ...(i)

r2=k2Ax2By=2.4 ...(ii)

r3=k2AxBy=0.6 ...(iii)

From (i) ÷ (iii)

12x1y=0.30.6

12x=12

x=1

(ii) ÷ (iii)

r2r3=2.40.6=2y

4=2y

y=2

The order of reaction w.r.t. A=1 and w.r.t. B=2

Total order of reaction =1+2=3

subject: Chemistry
q:
For certain chemical reaction X→Y, the rate of formation of product is plotted against the time as shown in the figure. The number of Correct statement/s from the following is _____ 

(A) Over all order of this reaction is one
(B) Order of this reaction can't be determined
(C) In region-I and III, the reaction is of first and zero order respectively
(D) In region-II, the reaction is of first order
(E) In region-II, the order of reaction is in the range of 0.1 to 0.9.
question:
For certain chemical reaction X→Y, the rate of formation of product is plotted against the time as shown in the figure. The number of Correct statement/s from the following is _____ 

(A) Over all order of this reaction is one
(B) Order of this reaction can't be determined
(C) In region-I and III, the reaction is of first and zero order respectively
(D) In region-II, the reaction is of first order
(E) In region-II, the order of reaction is in the range of 0.1 to 0.9.
solution: Rate of formation of product cannot be directly related to time, hence, can't predict order of reaction. Rate Vs time plot for first order reaction is not linear.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Rate law for a reaction between A and B is given by
$\mathrm{R}=\mathrm{k}[\mathrm{~A}]^{\mathrm{n}}[\mathrm{~B}]^{\mathrm{m}}$
If concentration of A is doubled and concentration of $B$ is halved from their initial value, the ratio of new rate of reaction to the initial rate of reaction $\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)$ is
question:
Rate law for a reaction between A and B is given by
$\mathrm{R}=\mathrm{k}[\mathrm{~A}]^{\mathrm{n}}[\mathrm{~B}]^{\mathrm{m}}$
If concentration of A is doubled and concentration of $B$ is halved from their initial value, the ratio of new rate of reaction to the initial rate of reaction $\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)$ is
solution:
$\mathrm{r}_1=\mathrm{k}[\mathrm{~A}]^{\mathrm{n}}[\mathrm{~B}]^{\mathrm{m}}$
Now A is doubled \& B is halved in concentration
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{r}_2=\mathrm{k} 2^{\mathrm{n}}[\mathrm{~A}]^{\mathrm{n}} \cdot \frac{[\mathrm{~B}]^{\mathrm{m}}}{2^{\mathrm{m}}}$
Now $\frac{r_2}{r_1}=2^{(n-m)}$

subject: Chemistry
q:
Consider the following first order gas phase reaction at constant temperature
\(\mathrm{A}(\mathrm{g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{~B}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{g})\)
If the total pressure of the gases is found to be 200 torr after \(23 \mathrm{sec}\). and 300 torr upon the complete decomposition of A after a very long time, then the rate constant of the given reaction is ______ \(\times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}\) (nearest integer)
[Given : \(\log _{10}(2)=0.301\)]
question:
Consider the following first order gas phase reaction at constant temperature
\(\mathrm{A}(\mathrm{g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{~B}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{g})\)
If the total pressure of the gases is found to be 200 torr after \(23 \mathrm{sec}\). and 300 torr upon the complete decomposition of A after a very long time, then the rate constant of the given reaction is ______ \(\times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}\) (nearest integer)
[Given : \(\log _{10}(2)=0.301\)]
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{A}(\mathrm{g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{~B}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{g}) \\ & \mathrm{P}_{23}=\mathrm{P}_0+2 \mathrm{x}=200 \\ & \mathrm{P}_{\infty}=3 \mathrm{P}_0=300 \\ & \mathrm{P}_0=100 \\ & \mathrm{~K}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{t}} \ln \frac{\mathrm{P}_{\infty}-\mathrm{P}_0}{\mathrm{P}_{\infty}-\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{t}}} \\ & \mathrm{K}=\frac{2.3}{23} \log \frac{300-100}{300-200} \\ & =\frac{2.3 \times 0.301}{23}=0.0301=3.01 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{sec}^{-1} \\ & \end{aligned}$
